insights: Cost Reduction goals across all departments show a near-perfect linear increase in average duration from ~185 days in Q1 to ~355 days in Q4, confirming a systemic, time-driven escalation rather than a department- or agent-specific anomaly.

The duration escalation is exclusive to the Cost Reduction category: Finance department goals in other categories remain stable at ~90–99 days across quarters with negligible time correlation (r = 0.065), ruling out Finance-wide operational degradation as a cause.

No structural or textual characteristics differentiate Cost Reduction goals from others — including priority, state, owner concentration, metric type, or description complexity — all showing statistically non-significant differences (p > 0.19) and negligible effect sizes, indicating the root cause lies outside goal-level attributes.

Cost Reduction goals exhibit significantly higher use of broad scope language (51.7% vs 31.7%) and time pressure framing (62.8% vs 45.7%), yet these linguistic patterns show no correlation with actual duration (r < 0.04), suggesting misalignment between goal articulation and execution planning rather than inherent complexity driving delays.

The near-identical quarterly duration trajectory across Finance and non-Finance Cost Reduction goals implies shared organizational practices — such as annual budget cycles, cross-departmental approval dependencies, or top-down strategic re-scoping — are synchronously inflating timelines for this category enterprise-wide.

With 58.6% of all goals still 'In Progress' and Cost Reduction goals having a *lower* In Progress rate (52.3%) than other categories (61.9%), the duration increase cannot be attributed to workflow stalling or status bias — instead, it reflects longer active execution or repeated re-planning phases.
summary: The escalating durations of 'Cost Reduction' goals are not a Finance department issue but an organization-wide phenomenon, uniformly affecting all departments with near-identical quarterly growth (~185 → ~355 days). This pattern is isolated to the Cost Reduction category — other goal types in Finance remain stable — and cannot be explained by structural factors (priority, state, ownership, metrics) or textual complexity. Instead, the consistent timing, linguistic framing (broad scope + time pressure), and absence of execution-related bottlenecks point to systemic drivers such as annual fiscal planning cycles, cascading re-prioritization, or insufficient scoping discipline during goal initiation. The next focus should be auditing the goal intake and quarterly review processes for Cost Reduction initiatives — particularly how targets are defined, resourced, and re-baselined — to identify where time inflation is being introduced operationally.
